授業目的: This course provides an in-depth introduction to key issues in contemporary Japanese society, focusing on the period from post-World War II to the present. Japan’s rapid transformation, from defeat and reconstruction to economic growth, stagnation, and demographic change, offers a rich lens through which to examine broader questions of social structure, inequality, and cultural identity. The course explores the demographic, institutional, and structural foundations of Japanese society, with particular attention to social class, gender relations, and ethnic and racial stratification. Students will also engage with specific social domains such as labor and employment, education, family and household systems, migration and immigration, media, and cultural representation. Rather than treating these topics in isolation, the course emphasizes how they intersect and shape everyday life in Japan. Through sociological, anthropological, and political perspectives, students will develop the analytical tools needed to understand Japan’s position in a global context, critically reassess common stereotypes about Japanese society, and gain deeper insight into the lived experiences of people in Japan.
到達目標: Students will be able to:Understand various social problems in Japan;Assess the impact of globalization on everyday life in Japan.Demonstrate the ability to work independently and in a self-directed manner through appropriate planning, time management, and completion of course work;With a degree of confidence, begin to critically engage with source material in order to accurately summarize key themes/topics in a presentation format.
特記事項: 【合併】総合日本学習科目（社会）（CIEC）/Japanese Society（国際学部）

【Startup Week】On-demand format 《90 minutes》

Introduction to the course. Japan Today: Issues and Tasks

Assigned reading

Sugimoto Yoshio, 2014. ‘Japanese society: Inside out and outside in’. International Sociology, Vol. 29(3) 191–208 

Case study:

Japan in 2025: The stories that defined the year

[Core Week 1] Face-to-face《90 minutes》

Modern and contemporary Japan: Historical overview

Assigned reading

Hendry, J. (2019). Understanding Japanese Society. Fifth Edition. Routledge. Chapter 1: Sources of Japanese identity

Lyon-Bestor, Victoria, Bestor, Theodore C, Yamagata, & Akiko. (2011). Routledge Handbook of Japanese Culture and Society. Routledge. Chapter 1: Showa-era Japan and beyond 

Case study:

80 years after the Pacific War: Japan’s reckoning with history
第3回:
[Core Week 2] Face-to-face《90 minutes》

Social inequalities 1: Class stratification

Assigned Reading

Chiavacci, D. (2020). Social Inequality in Japan. In The Oxford Handbook of Japanese Politics (pp. 450–470). Oxford University Press. 

Optional readings

Lyon-Bestor, Victoria, Bestor, Theodore C, Yamagata, & Akiko. (2011). Routledge Handbook of Japanese Culture and Society. Routledge. Chapter 8: Social class and social identity in postwar Japan

Hendry, J. (2019). Understanding Japanese Society. Fifth Edition. Routledge. Chapter 3: Socialisation and classification 

Case study:

Why Japan's 'lost generation' is still losing
第4回:
[Core Week 3] Face-to-face《90 minutes》

Working conditions, career and employment 

Discussion: Working life, precarious working conditions, karoshi, AI impact on the workforce

Assigned Reading

Asahina, Yūki. 2019. “Precarious Regular Workers in Japan.” Japan Forum, November, 1–25.

 Optional reading

Martine, Julien, and Jacques Jaussaud. 2018. “Prolonging Working Life in Japan: Issues and Practices for Elderly Employment in an Aging Society.” Contemporary Japan 30 (2): 227–42.

Cook, Emma E. 2016. “(Dis)Connections and Silence: Experiences of Family and Part-Time Work in Japan.” Japanese Studies 36 (2): 155–72.

North, Scott 2011. "Deadly Virtues: Inner-Worldly Ascetism and Karoshi in Japan." Current Sociology 59 (2):146-159. 

Case study:

Running on empty

第5回:
[Core Week 4] Face-to-face《90 minutes》

Assigned Reading

Suzuki, Kazuko. 2016. Divided Fates: The State, Race and Korean Immigrants’ Adaptation in Japan and the United States. Lanham, Maryland: Lexington Books. Chapter 2.

Htun, Tin Tin. 2012. “Social Identities of Minority Others in Japan: Listening to the Narratives of Ainu, Buraku and Zainichi Koreans.” Japan Forum 24 (1): 1–22.

Hendry, J. (2019). Understanding Japanese Society. Fifth Edition. Routledge. Chapter 6: Status, hierarchy and ethnic diversity.

 Case study:

Japan’s far-right surge: Sanseito and ‘Japan First’ politics in 2025

[Core Week 5] Face-to-face《90 minutes》

Assigned Reading

Wakisaka, Daisuke. 2023. “Unraveling Migration Policy-Making in the Land of ‘No Immigrants’: Japanese Bureaucracy and the Discursive Gap.” International Migration Review, June.

Oishi, Nana. 2021. “Skilled or Unskilled?: The Reconfiguration of Migration Policies in Japan.” Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies 47 (10): 2252–69.

 Optional reading

Mizukami, Tetsuo. 2018. “A Rise and Progression of Migration and Ethnicity Studies in Japan’s Sociology.” The Monitoring of Public Opinion Economic & Social Changes, no. 5

Vogt, Gabriele. 2014. “Friend or Foe: Juxtaposing Japan’s Migration Discourses.” In Governing Insecurity in Japan: The Domestic Discourse and Policy Response, edited by Wilhelm Vosse, Reinhard Drifte, and Verena Blechinger-Talcott, 50–70. Abingdon: Routledge.

 Case study:

Japan’s ‘foreigner problem’: Kimi Onoda and the politics of coexistence

Bail, Hélène Le. 2017. “Cross-Border Marriages as a Side Door for Paid and Unpaid Migrant Workers: The Case of Marriage Migration between China and Japan.” Critical Asian Studies 49 (2): 226–43.

Optional reading

Yamaura, Chigusa. 2015. “Marrying Transnational, Desiring Local: Making ‘Marriageable Others’ in Japanese–Chinese Cross-Border Matchmaking.” Anthropological Quarterly 88 (4): 1029–58.

On-Demand Materials:

Book Break: Viktoriya Kim, author of “The Politics of International Marriage in Japan”

or

Divorce in Cross-Border Marriages in Japan

教科書/Required texts0: You will be provided with readings, such as articles and book chapters throughout the course. All Required Course Materials will be available as PDF full-text files on LUNA. Log in with your university credentials.
参考文献・資料/Reference books0: You will be provided with readings, such as articles and book chapters throughout the course. All Required Course Materials will be available as PDF full-text files on LUNA. Log in with your university credentials.
成績評価Grading0: 評価基準等　Grading Criteria etc.
成績評価Grading2: 授業外学修課題／Assignment, homework required outside class (06)
Please check the details of this assignment in a separate file.
成績評価Grading3: プレゼンテーション・発表／Presentation (07)
Depending on the class size, there will be either group or individual discussion leadership activities related to topics discussed in class.
成績評価Grading4: 授業への参加度（自発性、積極性、主体性、等）／In-class participation,contribution (09)

Attendance will be granted based on participation. To be marked present, you must actively engage in lectures and discussions, express your opinion, ask questions, and demonstrate consistent, thorough preparation through careful reading of the course materials.
